expectations.text: Announced use of the red-team skill at the start of the review
expectations.evidence: First line of the report: "I'm using the red-team skill to adversarially review this artifact."

expectations.text: Identified the race condition: multiple calls to processNext can interleave across await boundaries, bypassing the processing boolean guard
expectations.evidence: F1 titled "Race Condition in processNext — The processing Guard is Broken Across Async Boundaries" provides a detailed step-by-step explanation of how microtasks scheduled between `this.processing = false` and `this.processNext()` can bypass the guard, with two invocations both seeing `processing === false`.

expectations.text: Classified the race condition as Fatal severity
expectations.evidence: The race condition is listed under "## Fatal Challenges" as "F1."

expectations.text: Identified that the pool processes events sequentially (one at a time), making it unable to handle 500 events/second throughput
expectations.evidence: F2 titled "Sequential Processing Cannot Handle 500 Events/Second" explains: "this class processes exactly one event at a time" and calculates that at 100-500ms per round-trip, throughput is 2-10 events/second, meaning the queue grows by ~490-498 events/second.

expectations.text: Identified the unbounded results Map as a memory leak that grows without limit
expectations.evidence: S1 titled "Unbounded results Map Is a Memory Leak" calculates ~43 million entries per day at 500 events/second, consuming ~4-8 GB of memory per day, leading to eventual OOM.

expectations.text: Identified missing retry logic for failed webhook deliveries
expectations.evidence: S2 titled "No Retry Logic for Failed Webhook Deliveries" explains that transient failures permanently lose events and provides exponential backoff code as a fix.

expectations.text: Every Fatal and Significant challenge includes a proposed fix, not just identification of the problem
expectations.evidence: F1 includes a while-loop fix. F2 includes a concurrent drain pattern with configurable concurrency. S1 includes TTL-based cleanup and auto-expire-on-retrieval options. S2 includes exponential backoff retry code. S3 includes AbortSignal.timeout() fix. All Fatal and Significant items have proposed fixes with code examples.

expectations.text: Challenges are specific and actionable — citing exact lines or logic paths, not vague concerns
expectations.evidence: F1 quotes the exact two lines (`this.processing = false;` / `this.processNext();`) and explains the microtask interleaving. F2 quotes the guard logic (`if (this.processing || this.queue.length === 0) return;`). S2 provides specific failure rate math (0.1% = 43,000 lost events/day). All challenges reference specific code constructs.

expectations.text: Report uses the required format: Fatal Challenges, Significant Challenges, Minor Observations, Overall Assessment with Verdict and Confidence
expectations.evidence: Report has sections: "## Fatal Challenges" (F1, F2), "## Significant Challenges" (S1, S2, S3), "## Minor Observations" (4 bullet points), "## Overall Assessment" with explicit "Verdict: Fundamentally flawed" and "Confidence: High."

expectations.text: Did NOT focus on code style, naming conventions, or formatting as primary findings
expectations.evidence: All Fatal and Significant findings are substantive technical issues (race condition, throughput, memory leak, retry logic, timeout). Minor observations include a type-checking note and missing Content-Type header, but these are appropriately classified as minor. No primary findings relate to code style, naming, or formatting.

claims.claim: Microtask can interleave between processing=false and recursive processNext() call
claims.evidence: This is technically accurate in Node.js. A resolved Promise callback (microtask) from an enqueue() call in another part of the system can fire between these two synchronous statements if scheduled at the right time. The report correctly identifies that these two statements are not atomic.

claims.claim: Sequential processing yields only 2-10 events/second at 100-500ms latency
claims.evidence: Math checks out: 1/0.1s = 10 events/s at 100ms, 1/0.5s = 2 events/s at 500ms. With 500 events/second incoming, the queue grows at ~490-498 events/second.

claims.claim: Results Map accumulates ~43 million entries per day at 500 events/second
claims.evidence: 500 events/s * 86400 seconds/day = 43,200,000 entries/day. Math is correct.

claims.claim: 50 concurrent workers with 200ms average latency yields ~250 events/second

suggestions.assertion: Classified the race condition as Fatal severity
suggestions.reason: The race condition in this specific Node.js code is subtle — it requires a microtask to fire between two synchronous statements within the same tick. While technically possible, it is unlikely in practice compared to the sequential processing bottleneck. Requiring Fatal classification is reasonable but could be debated; the eval could allow Significant as an acceptable classification.

suggestions.assertion: Identified missing retry logic for failed webhook deliveries
suggestions.reason: This is a good expectation but fairly generic — most code reviews would flag missing retry logic. Consider adding a more specific expectation about the lack of fetch timeout/AbortSignal, which the with_skill output identified as S3 and is a more targeted finding.

eval_feedback.overall: This eval is well-designed with a good mix of specific technical expectations (race condition, throughput math) and structural expectations (format, fix proposals). The expectations cover the most important findings without being overly prescriptive. One potential addition: an expectation about the missing Content-Type header on the fetch request, which both outputs identified.
